comments:
Summary and strengths:
This is a thorough, technically mature investigation of how to generalize prime implicates and prime implicants from propositional logic to the modal logic K (and by extension to Kn and ALC). The author: (i) carefully surveys multiple candidate syntactic definitions for literals/clauses/terms in K and evaluates them against a transparent list of desiderata; (ii) selects a principled definition (D4) and proves it satisfies many important properties (Finiteness, Covering, Equivalence, Implicant–Implicate Duality, Distribution); (iii) provides sound and complete algorithms for generating and recognizing prime implicates under that definition; and (iv) gives tight complexity results (including Pspace-completeness of recognition) and worst-case bounds on size/number of prime implicates, with matching lower bounds. The paper is well organized, repeatable (proofs in appendix), and contains informative examples and discussion of applications (abduction, knowledge compilation). Overall, it makes a substantial theoretical contribution to logical reasoning/knowledge representation.

Technical assessment and correctness:
The development is logically coherent. Definitions are motivated, the impossibility result (no definition can satisfy all desirable properties) is convincing, and the selection of D4 is well justified. Complexity analyses are thorough: reductions and matching lower/upper bounds are provided. Algorithms (GenPI, TestPI, Test3PI, and an improved space-efficient variant) are described at an appropriate level and their correctness/complexity are argued and supported by lemmas/theorems. I found no obvious flaws in reasoning in the presented text; proofs are delegated to the appendix as stated, which is appropriate for length.

Weaknesses and suggestions for improvement:
- Practicality: the paper itself acknowledges that the generation algorithm in its basic form is impractical due to double-exponential blow-up in worst case. While some optimizations and an incremental, single-space algorithm are discussed, the work would benefit from more concrete proposals or experimental evaluation of heuristics (e.g., incremental construction, early pruning, syntactic restrictions, or use of SAT/SMT/back-end reasoners) to assess practical feasibility on non-pathological inputs.
- Examples and use-cases: the motivating applications (abduction, knowledge compilation) are discussed at a conceptual level. A small running example of abductive explanation generation (showing how prime implicants look in practice), or a brief empirical case study (even toy-scale) would strengthen the connection to applications.
- Global consequence and TBox-style reasoning: the paper notes that global consequence is harder and leaves it as future work. A bit more elaboration on how the presented techniques fail or could be adapted for global consequence / TBox interpolation would be helpful for readers in DL communities.
- Readability: the paper is dense and technical; while notation is consistent, some longer proofs or algorithm descriptions could be supported with more intuition or diagrams for readers less familiar with modal tableaux/description-logic encodings.

Recommendation to the community and future work:
This paper should be read and cited by researchers interested in modal/description logics, knowledge compilation, and logical abduction. It opens multiple directions: (i) practical implementations and heuristics for prime implicate generation in modal DLs; (ii) extension to other modal/description logics (e.g., with frame properties, logics of knowledge/belief, expressive DLs used in the Semantic Web); (iii) study of prime implicates under global consequence or with bounded modal depth; (iv) integration into DL tooling for explanation and compilation. The theoretical foundations here are strong and will support such applied work.

Minor comments:
- The main body omits proofs for readability; this is acceptable but the appendixed proofs should be carefully typeset and cross-referenced (I did not check the appendix formatting here).  
- A short table listing which of P1–P7 each definition satisfies (summarized) would help quick scanning (the text indicates such a figure exists).  

Overall assessment: rigorous, original, and impactful theoretical work with clear relevance to knowledge representation and reasoning. I recommend acceptance and encourage follow-up work that addresses practical algorithms/heuristics and experiments.
